section_outline.heading: What Claude Cowork Can and Cannot Automate
Skills, Plugins, and Agents: The Three Building Blocks
Worked Example: Tracy Evaluates Her Status Report Workflow

running_example.scenario_title: Weekly project status report from scattered sources
entities: Tracy, Senior Program Manager at Advisor360°
Jira board for the project (tickets by status)
Confluence project page (change log, decision log)
Google Meet stand-up transcripts (two per week)
artifacts: RSTRM scorecard for the weekly status report workflow
Draft executive status report (produced by the automation; reviewed by Tracy)
Detailed supporting notes organized by source
Source citations linking each report section to its originating system

worked_example_seed.problem_statement: Tracy wants to determine whether her weekly project status report workflow — currently a 2.5-hour manual process drawing from Jira, Confluence, Slack, and Google Meet transcripts — is a strong candidate for Claude Cowork automation. She applies the RSTRM framework criterion by criterion to produce a scored evaluation and a design verdict.
worked_example_seed.given_state: Tracy's current weekly reporting process for one of her two concurrent programs at Advisor360°. Sources: Jira board (closed/blocked tickets), Confluence project page (recent edits, decision log), Slack project channel (past week's messages), two Google Meet stand-up transcripts. Output format: five-section executive summary (Accomplishments, Risks, Upcoming Milestones, Decisions Needed, Key Discussions) plus detailed supporting notes. Current time cost: approximately 2.5 hours per report.

solution_steps.action: Tracy confirms the report runs every Monday without exception for both programs.
solution_steps.why: Calendar-based trigger; consistent frequency; runs approximately 50 times per year per program.

solution_steps.action: Tracy verifies that all four source systems produce consistent-format data and that the output template has been stable for eight months.
solution_steps.why: Jira exports a structured ticket list; Confluence pages have a stable layout; Slack messages follow consistent decision/blocker patterns; transcripts follow Google Meet format; output template is fixed.

solution_steps.action: Tracy estimates the time savings: 2.5 hours per report × 2 programs × 50 weeks = 250 hours per year recoverable.
solution_steps.why: 250 hours per year is a material savings. Even at 60% automation success that is 150 hours recovered annually.

solution_steps.action: Tracy writes out explicit decision rules for each source system — what qualifies as an Accomplishment, a Risk, a Decision, or an Action Item.
solution_steps.why: Rules are articulable and consistent; Slack messages require a first-pass classification by Claude with final judgment by Tracy at the human-in-the-loop checkpoint.

solution_steps.action: Tracy maps six discrete steps: retrieve Jira, retrieve Confluence, retrieve Slack, retrieve transcripts, synthesize draft, pause for review.
solution_steps.why: Six steps with intermediate artifacts; agent orchestration is appropriate; a single skill would be insufficient.

worked_example_seed.final_state: RSTRM scorecard: all five criteria rated Strong. Verdict: Tracy's weekly status report workflow is a high-confidence automation candidate. Recommended architecture: an agent orchestrating a status-report skill and four plugins (Jira, Confluence, Slack, Google Meet), with a human-in-the-loop checkpoint before the report is sent.

decision_points.description: Occasional skip weeks (twice per year during sprint transitions) — handled by encoding a skip flag in the skill rather than disabling the automation, so the automation runs and self-documents the skip.
Slack messages are free-text but follow consistent patterns — structured at the RSTRM level means consistently extractable, not machine-formatted. Pattern-based extraction is sufficient.
Stakeholder message classification (real blocker vs. casual concern) stays with Tracy at the human-in-the-loop checkpoint; Claude performs a first-pass classification that Tracy validates.

Stale Jira data risk (developers close tickets on Tuesday not Friday) — mitigated by running the automation Tuesday morning rather than Monday morning, with a report cutoff time parameter encoded in the skill.

glossary_delta.definition: A saved, reusable instruction set that Claude executes on demand. A skill encodes what inputs to expect, what format to produce, and what constraints to apply — equivalent to a written SOP that Claude reads and executes rather than a human performing manually. Skills do not connect to external systems independently; they call plugins to retrieve live data.

A connector that gives Claude read or write access to an external business system. Plugins handle authentication, querying, and data formatting, exposing a consistent interface that skills can invoke. Plugins require explicit authorization and can be revoked at any time.

A Claude configuration that autonomously plans and executes a sequence of steps — calling skills, invoking plugins, evaluating intermediate results, and deciding what to do next — until it reaches a defined stopping condition or a human-in-the-loop checkpoint. Appropriate for multi-step workflows; single-step tasks use skills.

A five-criterion checklist for evaluating automation candidates: Repetitive, Structured, Time-consuming, Rules-based, Multi-step. A task scoring Strong on all five criteria is a high-confidence automation candidate. Two or more Weak ratings indicate the task scope should be redesigned or deferred.

A deliberate pause in an automated workflow where Tracy reviews and approves the automation's output before the next action — such as sending a report, applying a Gmail label, or moving a file. Required for all high-stakes outputs and a non-negotiable design element of every agent workflow in this course.

chapter_pitfalls.misconception: Claude can impose consistent structure on inconsistent inputs.
chapter_pitfalls.why_wrong: Claude produces output consistent with the patterns it finds in its inputs. Inconsistent templates produce inconsistent drafts. Claude is a pattern-learner, not a format enforcer.
chapter_pitfalls.correction: Standardize the output template before automating the workflow that produces it. Use the template manually for two or three cycles before encoding it in a skill.
chapter_pitfalls.diagnostic: If the first three automation runs each produce reports in a different structure, the cause is an underspecified or inconsistent output template. Fix the template; rerun.

chapter_pitfalls.misconception: Once the status report skill is built, it can send the report automatically without Tracy reviewing it.
chapter_pitfalls.why_wrong: A status report reaching leadership with an error damages Tracy's credibility. The automation cannot assess its own accuracy against her professional judgment. Human review before distribution is non-negotiable for high-stakes outputs.
chapter_pitfalls.correction: Automate the draft; require human review before distribution. Time savings come from eliminating data-gathering and synthesis labor, not from removing Tracy from the approval chain.
chapter_pitfalls.diagnostic: If the temptation is to forward an automation output without reading it, the human-in-the-loop checkpoint has been skipped in practice. Restore it.

chapter_pitfalls.misconception: An agent is needed to pull Jira data — a plugin alone is insufficient.
chapter_pitfalls.why_wrong: Pulling Jira data is a plugin function. An agent is not needed for a single retrieval step. Confusing the three building blocks leads to over-engineered first automations that are difficult to debug.
chapter_pitfalls.correction: Start with the simplest combination that accomplishes the goal. Single retrieval uses a plugin. Transformation tasks use a skill. Multi-step workflows that retrieve, synthesize, and deliver use an agent coordinating a skill and one or more plugins. Build complexity incrementally.
chapter_pitfalls.diagnostic: If building the first automation feels overwhelming, it is too complex for a first attempt. Reduce to a single skill with manually supplied input data. Add plugins and agent orchestration in Chapters 8 and 11.

retrieval_checkpoints.prompt: What is the single characteristic that separates the automatable tasks from the human-judgment-required tasks in the suitability table? Write your answer before reading the revealed answer.
Without looking at the table, describe in one sentence each: what a skill does, what a plugin does, and what an agent does. Then check your answers against the definitions in section 5.
List all five RSTRM criteria from memory — just the letters and the words they represent. Do not look back at section 6.

Name three characteristics that make a program management task a strong automation candidate. Then describe the difference between a Claude skill and a Claude plugin, with one example of each from Tracy's reporting workflow.
List all five RSTRM criteria and state whether Tracy's weekly status report workflow satisfies each one, and why.

reflection_prompts.prompt: The distinction between skills, plugins, and agents is clean on a diagram but blurry in practice. Which of the three did you find hardest to pin down? What would you tell a colleague who asked you to explain that one building block in thirty seconds?

Before reading this chapter, how did you think about the boundary between what automation can do and what requires your professional judgment? Has that boundary moved — and if so, in which direction? What one insight caused the shift?

Which one task in your current workflow would you score using the RSTRM framework before Chapter 2? Draft the scorecard now — criterion by criterion, with a rating and one-line justification for each — and bring it to the Chapter 2 exercises.

diagrams.name: three-layers-mental-model
diagrams.alt_text: A flowchart with three labeled subgraph boxes arranged vertically — Layer 1 (Skills) containing a Claude Skill box, Layer 2 (Plugins) containing Jira, Confluence, Slack, and Gmail plugin boxes, and Layer 3 (Agents) containing an Agent box. Arrows from the Claude Skill box point down to the Jira, Confluence, and Slack plugin boxes labeled 'calls.' An arrow from the Agent box points to the Skill box labeled 'orchestrates,' another arrow from the Agent box points to the Gmail plugin box labeled 'orchestrates,' and a final arrow from the Agent box points right to a Human-in-the-Loop Checkpoint box labeled 'pauses at.'

candidate_misconceptions: Skills connect directly to external systems without a plugin
Agents are required for any multi-step task, including single-source retrievals
Human-in-the-loop checkpoints can be removed once the automation is proven reliable
A task that is not rules-based can still be automated reliably by Claude
The RSTRM framework requires all five criteria to be rated Strong before a task is automatable
candidate_scenarios: Tracy wants to automatically pull last week's closed Jira tickets into a list — classify as skill, plugin, or agent
Tracy wants to convert a raw Google Meet transcript into a structured summary — classify as skill, plugin, or agent
Tracy wants a workflow that reads Jira, reads Confluence, drafts a report, and waits for her review — classify as skill, plugin, or agent
Tracy scores a task as Repetitive=Strong, Structured=Partial, Time-consuming=Strong, Rules-based=Weak, Multi-step=Strong — should she build the automation now?
An automation produces a draft status report. Tracy's assistant suggests forwarding it to leadership without Tracy reviewing it. What is wrong with this suggestion?
